Algorithms covered: None — language learning; cited as a gamification model rather than a CS/algorithms tool
Engagement actions (clicking dragging wr: Tapping answer tiles; typing responses; drag-to-match; listening and speaking exercises; swiping through lessons
Value presentation (colors shapes labels: XP bar; streak flame counter; heart (life) system; gem/lingot currency; league leaderboard; color-coded correctness feedback (green/red); animated mascot (Duo)
Null handling: Wrong answers deduct a heart; heart depletion ends the session — learner must restart or refill hearts; creates an explicit cost for errors; no CS-sense of null
Adaptivity / adjustable (levels speed se: Yes — spaced repetition engine (SuperMemo-derived) surfaces weaker items more frequently; lesson difficulty adapts to historical accuracy; Hearts pace influenced by user subscription tier (free vs. Plus)
Speed control?: No explicit speed control; optional timed challenge mode available as a variant
Value size control? (array size bar size: No — content granularity is fixed per lesson; no user-adjustable parameters
Student engagement / learning notes: One of the most-studied gamified learning platforms. High DAU and streak retention are well-documented. However research distinguishes engagement from acquisition: Loewen et al. (2019) found moderate gains for beginners but weak evidence for advanced learners. The streak mechanic has been linked to anxiety and minimal-effort completion behavior (doing just enough to preserve the streak) — a cautionary design note for any streak or progress system in OpenLearnQuest.
Advantages (objective): (1) Extremely high retention and daily engagement documented at scale; (2) XP + streak + leaderboard loop is a concrete and well-understood gamification benchmark; (3) Spaced repetition integration is empirically grounded; (4) Free tier lowers access barriers; (5) Mobile-first design supports microlearning
Limitations (objective): (1) Not a CS or algorithms tool — relevance is purely as a gamification design model; (2) Gamification elements can overshadow learning outcomes (engagement ≠ mastery); (3) Streak anxiety and minimal-effort completion are documented negative side-effects; (4) Adaptive engine is proprietary and not replicable in open-source contexts; (5) Thin evidence that gamification causes learning gains independently of other factors
Tags: gamification; spaced repetition; streak mechanics; adaptive learning; mobile learning; language learning; engagement design; cautionary model; extrinsic motivation

Algorithms covered: CS fundamentals; logic and reasoning; algorithmic thinking (sorting/searching concepts); introductory data structures; discrete mathematics; probability — framed as guided problem sets rather than code-level simulations
Engagement actions (clicking dragging wr: Clicking interactive diagram elements; selecting and submitting answers to multi-step problems; manipulating sliders in visual explainers; guided worked-example stepping (click to reveal next step)
Value presentation (colors shapes labels: Clean minimal UI; step-by-step problem cards with embedded diagrams; color-coded answer feedback; progress arc per course; no avatar or currency system — visual reward is course completion percentage and unlocking the next problem
Null handling: Incorrect answers surface a targeted explanation of why the choice was wrong and redirect the learner to re-attempt or view a hint; no punitive penalty system (no lives/hearts); learner can attempt freely
Adaptivity / adjustable (levels speed se: Yes — learning paths adapt based on a diagnostic quiz at course entry; within-course difficulty scales with completion rate; no real-time speed or size adjustment during problem interaction
Speed control?: No explicit speed control; entirely self-paced
Value size control? (array size bar size: No — problem parameters are fixed per exercise; no user-adjustable input size
Student engagement / learning notes: Takes a problem-first constructivist approach: learners encounter a problem before receiving formal instruction — aligning with productive failure research (Kapur 2016). Distinct from passive video MOOCs and purely game-based tools like CodeCombat. For algorithms Brilliant offers conceptual coverage (e.g. how merge sort works) but no code-level drag-and-drop or execution environment. Engagement is sustained by intellectual curiosity rather than extrinsic loops — a useful contrast to Duolingo's extrinsic-heavy model. Formal peer-reviewed evidence on Brilliant specifically is sparse; most comes from internal/company-reported metrics or adjacent problem-based learning literature.
Advantages (objective): (1) High-quality STEM content with genuine algorithm and CS coverage; (2) Problem-first approach aligns with productive failure and active learning theory; (3) Interactive diagrams make abstract concepts tangible without requiring a coding environment; (4) No punitive failure mechanic reduces learner anxiety; (5) Structured curriculum with clear learning path supports self-directed learners; (6) Available on web and mobile
Limitations (objective): (1) Subscription paywall limits access equity (free tier covers only a subset of content); (2) Very limited peer-reviewed outcome evidence — mostly company-reported or anecdotal; (3) No code execution or pseudocode assembly — conceptual only not procedural skill-building; (4) Algorithm visualizations are largely static diagrams rather than dynamic step-through animations (contrast with VisuAlgo); (5) No collaborative or social features; (6) Not open-source
Tags: STEM education; problem-based learning; productive failure; interactive learning; algorithms; data structures; adaptive learning path; subscription platform; constructivism; contrast case; intrinsic motivation
